reviews.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch does not address mkfs.btrfs performance and instead pointed to a separate pull request for improvements.
reviews.raw_body:
mkfs patches for this: https://github.com/kdave/btrfs-progs/pull/1096

On 20/03/2026 12.50 pm, Mark Harmstone wrote:

2026-03-20.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl (input/output control) called BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS, which allows userspace to query the on-disk checksums for a file without needing to read the data itself. The ioctl takes an argument specifying the offset and length of interest, as well as a buffer to store the results. The kernel returns a struct containing information about whether the checksum is available, and if so, what type it is (e.g., sparse, preallocated, or compressed). This feature is primarily intended for speeding up mkfs.btrfs --rootdir operations, particularly when using the --reflink option, which can result in significant performance improvements.

reviews.author: Qu Wenruo
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed concerns about the handling of hole and preallocated ranges in the BTRFS_GET_CSUMS_SPARSE type, suggesting that they should be treated differently due to their distinct implications for mkfs behavior. Specifically, holes should not create any extent instead of writing zero, while preallocated ranges can indicate no csums to allow mkfs to distinguish between them and change to zero writes accordingly.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested adding a special flag to indicate when csums are skipped for compressed extents, instead of using NO_CSUMS, to improve distinguishability between holes and compressed data.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested combining btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry and real buffer for csum into one structure, and removing redundant offset/length fields from the entry structure.
reviews.raw_body:
I'm not sure if it's a good idea to put hole and preallocated range into 
the same BTRFS_GET_CSUMS_SPARSE.

Although both means there is no csum, hole case means there is really no 
data extent, thus we should not create any extent instead of writing zero.

For preallocated, indicating it has no CSUM can allow mkfs to 
distinguish hole and preallocated, thus change to zero writes to 
prealloc, which is faster and make the resulted fs more aligned to the 
source dir.


And for EOF checks, I think we don't need to bother that much, aka, just 
let it return the regular results.

My assumption is, the mkfs shouldn't pass a range completely beyond the 
round_up(i_size), as non-reflink rootdir population would always read 
out the content of the inode from the host fs.
Thus we won't really read beyond the inode size.

---

I agree with the skip of compressed extents, but I'd prefer to have a 
special flag to indicate that, other than NO_CSUMS.

Or mkfs is unable to distinguish hole and compressed extents.

[...]

---

From the progs usage, it is always a single btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry 
at the beginning of buf[], then real buffer for csum, can we just 
combine both structures into one?

Furthermore, since we only query one extent at one time, the 
offset/length are more or less duplicated between args and entry structure.

We can just save the length into the args without the need for entry 
members (except the type).

Thanks,
Qu

2026-03-21.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl (input/output control) called BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS, which allows userspace to query the on-disk checksums for a file without needing to read the data itself. The ioctl takes an argument specifying the offset and length of interest, as well as a buffer to store the results. The kernel returns a struct containing information about whether the checksum is available, and if so, what type it is (e.g., sparse, preallocated, or compressed). This feature is primarily intended for speeding up mkfs.btrfs --rootdir operations, particularly when using the --reflink option, which can result in significant performance improvements.

reviews.author: Qu Wenruo
reviews.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested moving hole/preallocation/compression detection from kernel space to user space by utilizing SEEK_DATA/SEEK_HOLE flags of lseek() and fiemap, which would simplify the get csum ioctl and align it with btrfs_lookup_csums_bitmap().
reviews.raw_body:
After more investigation, I think we can put the 
hole/preallocation/compression detection into the user space.

The hole detection is already pending for merge, mostly through 
SEEK_DATA/SEEK_HOLE flags of lseek():

  https://github.com/kdave/btrfs-progs/pull/1097

I'm planning to implement preallocation detection through fiemap, which 
also allows us to detect compressed range and skip them for your case.

With all those features implemented in progs, we can further simplify 
the get csum ioctl, to something more aligned to 
btrfs_lookup_csums_bitmap().

We do not need to bother why there is no checksum for some ranges, that 
will be handled by progs first, we only need to return all the checksums 
found for the specified range.

And as an extra safenet, use some bitmap inthe ioctl structure to 
indicate which ranges have checksum and which doesn't.

This will definitely simplify the ioctl as we only need to do csum tree 
lookup, no need to bother anything in the subvolume tree.

Thanks,
Qu

reviews.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged a naming issue in the ioctl type for sparse extents and agreed to rename it to something like 'ZERO' or similar, indicating no further revision planned.

The author acknowledged that returning checksums for compressed extents would leak information about other files, and explained that this is why encoded read requires root access.
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks Qu.

"SPARSE" is probably a bad name for it. It probably should be "ZERO" or 
somesuch. The point is to tell userspace not to waste time calculating 
csums, but use the precomputed values because the data would be zero 
(for whatever reason).

---

Unfortunately this won't work. You have to explicitly filter out 
compressed extents, and identifying these requires checking the FS tree. 
The reason is that they may be "bookended", and you would leak 
information about other files if you returned the whole of the csums for 
the compressed extent. This is the reason why encoded read needs root.

2026-03-25.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl (input/output control) called BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS, which allows userspace to query the on-disk checksums for a file without needing to read the data itself. The ioctl takes an argument specifying the offset and length of interest, as well as a buffer to store the results. The kernel returns a struct containing information about whether the checksum is available, and if so, what type it is (e.g., sparse, preallocated, or compressed). This feature is primarily intended for speeding up mkfs.btrfs --rootdir operations, particularly when using the --reflink option, which can result in significant performance improvements.

reviews.author: Qu Wenruo
reviews.summary:
reviewer pointed out that the new ioctl can be implemented using fiemap, which already has a flag to indicate compressed file extents

Reviewer noted that btrfs_ioctl_get_csums() will not encounter bookend extents during a fiemap call and thus the check for BTRFS_GET_CSUMS_SPARSE is unnecessary.
reviews.raw_body:
That's done by progs through fiemap. There will be a flag ENCODED for 
compressed file extents.

---

Nope, for the fiemap call, we will never reach any bookend extents.

Thanks,
Qu

2026-03-26.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl (input/output control) called BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS, which allows userspace to query the on-disk checksums for a file without needing to read the data itself. The ioctl takes an argument specifying the offset and length of interest, as well as a buffer to store the results. The kernel returns a struct containing information about whether the checksum is available, and if so, what type it is (e.g., sparse, preallocated, or compressed). This feature is primarily intended for speeding up mkfs.btrfs --rootdir operations, particularly when using the --reflink option, which can result in significant performance improvements.
